name: Jelly Roll Morton
description: American ragtime and jazz pianist, bandleader and composer (1890–1941)
abstract: Ferdinand Joseph LaMothe (né Lemott, later Morton; c. September 20, 1890 – July 10, 1941), known professionally as Jelly Roll Morton, was an American blues and jazz pianist, bandleader, and composer of Louisiana Creole descent.

works_created.t: I Thought I Heard Buddy Bolden Say

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Wilber
about_quotes.evidence: I was realizing that there were the “hot” swing bands and then there was more commercial bands... my discovery of earlier jazz really was because the big companies, Columbia and Victor, started re-issuing the old records of Louis Armstrong, Jelly Roll Morton, Bix Beiderbecke, the people of the ‘20s.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Wilber interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Now, I’ve outlived most everybody [laughs]. I’m ninety-one... Then I start talking about Jelly Roll Morton, everybody talking about Jelly Roll Morton. Duke Ellington said, “Jelly Roll Morton, Jelly Roll Morton, all he could do was brag about himself.” Yeah, but you copied him. He copied Jelly Roll M
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jules Cahn
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] No, I'm referring to an interview with Jelly Roll Morton, where Jelly talks about... He specifically... Talks about... I'm getting some feedback here. Wait. Jelly specifically talks about the... About the spa boys and people actually getting hurt on Mardi Gras Day.

about_quotes.speaker: George Avakian
about_quotes.evidence: the division even stayed on records for quite a long time, although it was broken in small ways like Jelly Roll Morton playing with the New Orleans Rhythm Kings on that record in 1922 I believe or ‘23.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Avakian interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 21, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: I’m playing some Jelly Roll piece like – Jelly Roll Morton piece like Grandpa’s Spells or something... Jelly Roll Morton was one of the great composers and arrangers of the ’20s.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: So I went over and she said, 'Mr. Morton. This is the famous Jelly Roll Morton.' ... Anyway, I met him, and he talked to me about how long I'd been playing and all that.
